questions.question: Why does a longer HLS segment duration hurt seek latency?
options: When the user seeks to a position mid-segment, the player must download from the segment start or wait for the correct segment boundary, adding delay proportional to segment length.
Longer segments require a different codec that does not support random access.
The CDN cannot cache segments longer than 4 seconds.
Longer segments force the client to use a lower bitrate profile.
questions.explanation: Seeking in HLS-style streaming lands on a segment boundary. Longer segments mean the nearest boundary is farther from the requested position, increasing seek time.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of per-title encoding at Netflix?
options: To use lower bitrates for simpler content while maintaining the same perceived quality, reducing storage and bandwidth without a visible quality change.
To add more bitrate levels to the encoding ladder for every title uniformly.
To support a wider range of devices by adding more codec variants.
To encrypt each title with a unique DRM key at the codec level.
questions.explanation: Per-title encoding adapts the bitrate-quality trade-off to the specific complexity of each title. A nature documentary needs more bits than a static talking-head interview at the same quality.

questions.question: Why should ABR algorithms switch down aggressively but switch up conservatively?
options: A sudden quality drop mid-scene is more disruptive to the viewer than a delayed upgrade to higher quality.
Switching up is more expensive computationally for the client player.
CDNs penalize frequent upward switches with higher latency.
Switching down is a no-op and does not require a new segment request.
questions.explanation: Users notice quality drops far more than they notice quality not improving immediately. Conservative upward switching also prevents thrashing between bitrates during brief bandwidth spikes.

questions.question: What is the safest way to prevent a corrupt encode from reaching subscribers?
options: Validate segment existence and playback integrity before publishing the manifest, and never publish a manifest that references unvalidated segments.
Rely on CDN cache TTLs to expire the corrupt segment within minutes.
Accept the corrupt encode but flag it in the database for later replacement.
Route only 1% of traffic to a new encode before full rollout.
questions.explanation: The manifest is the gate. Once it is published, clients will request segments. Validating before manifest publication is the only reliable pre-subscriber gate.

questions.question: Which failure mode is unique to DRM packaging compared to plain encoding?
options: The key service is a critical external dependency — if it is unavailable, packaging cannot complete and segments must not be published unencrypted.
DRM packaging always doubles the storage requirement for every segment.
DRM errors only affect mobile devices, not smart TVs.
The codec must be re-selected when the key service is unavailable.
questions.explanation: DRM packaging depends on a key service at packaging time. If the key service is down, the correct mitigation is to queue and retry — never to publish unencrypted content.

questions.question: How does parallelizing encoding across many workers affect the encoding pipeline design?
options: Work must be split into independently encodable units (segments or GOP groups), results must be reassembled, and the scheduler must handle worker failures without re-encoding the whole title.
Parallel encoding requires all workers to share a single encoding state in memory.
Parallelism only helps for 4K content; lower resolutions encode fast enough sequentially.
The encoding ladder must be reduced to one codec to enable parallelism.
questions.explanation: Parallel encoding is only efficient if work units are independent. Segments or GOP groups satisfy this. The scheduler must track which segments are done and reassemble the output without full-title retries.

questions.question: What is the strongest argument for using versioned manifest URLs instead of cache-busting headers?
options: Versioned URLs let CDN caches retain old manifests for in-progress sessions while new sessions immediately receive the updated manifest, preventing disruption to current viewers.
Versioned URLs are required by the HLS specification for VOD content.
Cache-busting headers are not supported by the Open Connect CDN layer.
Versioned URLs automatically expire old segments from the CDN cache.
questions.explanation: CDN cache-busting invalidates for everyone. Versioned URLs let existing sessions continue on the old manifest while new sessions pick up the new one — a much safer rollout.
